name: chamomile
mechanism: Proposed activity includes flavonoids (for example apigenin) and volatile constituents with anti-inflammatory and possible GABA-modulating effects. These are mechanistic hypotheses with partial clinical support rather than definitive human mechanism proof.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference:
nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
contraindications: Use caution with prescription medications and chronic conditions; monitor for intolerance.
safetyNotes: Often well tolerated in food and tea amounts. Allergic reactions can occur, especially in people sensitive to ragweed-family plants. Concentrated supplements can cause side effects and should be used cautiously in pregnancy and in people with complex medication regimens.
description: Chamomile (usually Matricaria chamomilla/recutita preparations) is used as tea and extracts for gastrointestinal discomfort, sleep support, and mild anxiety. Clinical evidence is mixed but suggests potential benefit for some mild anxiety and dyspepsia-related symptoms; it is not a replacement for urgent or severe-condition care.
dosage: No universally accepted standardized dose across all indications. Tea and extract dosing varies by preparation; follow labeled concentrations and clinician advice.

interactions: Potential interaction concern with anticoagulants/antiplatelet drugs due to additive bleeding risk in susceptible patients.
Sedative effects may be additive with CNS depressants; monitor for excess drowsiness.
Check for product-specific cautions because chamomile supplement formulations vary widely.
preparation: Common forms include infusion (tea), liquid extracts, and topical preparations. Clinical studies use non-uniform extract preparations, limiting direct dose comparisons.
region: Native to Europe and Western Asia; now widely cultivated in Europe, North America, and other temperate regions.
therapeuticUses: traditional support for mild digestive comfort
sideEffects: mild gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, or loose stool)
allergic rash or itching in sensitive individuals
summary: Chamomile is used for mild digestive discomfort, sleep complaints, and anxiety symptoms; evidence is strongest for mild short-term symptom relief and remains modest overall.
